title: × Sorbaronia alpina
intro: Sorbaronia alpina, also known as Mountain Ash, is a deciduous shrub or small tree that belongs to the rose family (Rosaceae) and is native to Europe and parts of Asia. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in the treatment of various ailments such as colds, fever, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Sorbaronia alpina include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Mountain Ash include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to relieve symptoms of colds and flu, as well as to support the immune system.
taxonomy: × Sorbaronia alpina is a hybrid of Sorbus aucuparia and Pyrus alba, both belonging to the family Rosaceae. As a hybrid, it is often classified as an intergeneric hybrid, combining characteristics from both parent species. The classification of × Sorbaronia alpina is not universally agreed upon and can vary depending on the source. Some sources may consider it a distinct species, while others may view it as a variant of one of its parent species. The classification of × Sorbaronia alpina can be complex and may be subject to ongoing debate.
uses: × Sorbaronia alpina, also known as alder buckthorn, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits. Its berries and leaves are believed to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat digestive issues and as a natural remedy for fever. However, more research is needed to confirm its medicinal uses. The plant's berries are also edible and are sometimes consumed fresh or used in jams and preserves.
